# **Cloud Mastery: Pawa-ring AI-Powered Apps**

### **Session 1 :** The Secure AI Backend & Data Foundation

**Summary:** This session focuses on setting up the core, secure infrastructure for an AI-powered application. Participants will connect a NestJS backend to a Cloud SQL (MySQL) database, establish robust CI/CD pipelines using Workload Identity Federation and GitHub Actions for deployment to Cloud Run, and set up a foundation for data replication to BigQuery for advanced AI/ML analytics with Vertex AI.

### What you’ll learn

Goal: To connect a NestJS backend to a Cloud SQL (MySQL) database, build the core AI API routes, establish separate and secure CI/CD pipelines, and replicate data to BigQuery for analytics.

Core Technologies:

* IDE : Such as VS Code or Antigravity IDE   
* Backend: NestJS (Node.js Framework)  
* Frontend: Next.js  
* Database: Cloud SQL for MySQL  
* Data Warehouse: BigQuery  
* AI/ML: Vertex AI (Gemini Models, Agent Builder, BigQuery ML)  
* Deployment: Cloud Run with GitHub Actions & Workload Identity Federation  
    
    
  


### Requirements

1. Install Git 

   ### **Windows**

   **1.Download the Installer:**  
   Go to the official Git website at [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com/download/win) and download the **64-bit Git for Windows Setup**.  
   **2.Run the Installer: \***Stick to the defaults.  
   Open the downloaded .exe file. The installer will present *a lot* of options and checkboxes. As a beginner, **leave everything on the default settings** and just keep clicking "Next" until you hit "Install".  
   **3.Verify the Installation:**  
   Once finished, open your Start menu, search for **Command Prompt**, and open it. Type the following command and press Enter:

```shell
git -v
```

   ### **macOS**

   Apple actually includes a quick way to install Git right out of the box.

   **1.Open the Terminal:**

   Press Cmd \+ Space to open Spotlight search, type **Terminal**, and hit Enter.

   **2.Trigger the Installation:**Using Apple's built-in tools.

   In the Terminal window, type the following command and press Enter:

```shell
git --version
```

   **3.Accept the Prompt:**

   If Git isn't installed yet, a pop-up window will appear asking if you want to install the "Command Line Developer Tools." Click **Install** and agree to the terms.

   **4.Verify:**

   Once the installation finishes, go back to your Terminal, type git \--version again, and you should now see the version number printed.

   ### **Linux (Ubuntu)**

   Linux is built for development, so installing Git is just a couple of quick commands.

   **1.Open the Terminal:**

   Press Ctrl \+ Alt \+ T to open your terminal window.

   **2.Update Package Lists:**Always do this before installing new software.

   Type this command and press Enter (it will ask for your computer's password):

```shell
sudo apt update
```

   **3.Install Git:**

   Once the update finishes, run:

```shell
sudo apt install git
If it asks "Do you want to continue? [Y/n]", press Y and Enter.
```

   **4.Verify the Installation:**

   Check that it worked by running:

```shell
git --version
```

2. Setup Gcloud SDK  
- Go to this link to install gcloud cli for your local machine: [https://docs.cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install-sdk](https://docs.cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install-sdk) 

2.1 Run to initialize gcloud: 

```json
 gcloud init
```

	![][image1]  
2.2 Don't set a default zone. **Enter 1**  
**![][image2]**  
2.3 For default region set \`us-central1\`. **Enter 36**  
**![][image3]**

3. 🐙 Github account  
4. ☁️ Valid Google Workspace  email to access the resources: This were shared to you via email   
5. Install Antigravity IDE or your preferred IDE 

   ### **Windows**

   **1.Download the Installer:**  
   Head over to the official site at [antigravity.google](https://antigravity.google) and download the Windows version of the **Antigravity IDE**.  
   **2.Run the Setup:**  
   Open the downloaded installer. Click through the initial setup and accept the Security and Data Use policy.  
   **3.Authenticate:**Required for access.  
   When prompted, log in with your Google account. The platform is free for individual use with a personal Gmail account.  
   **4.Configure your Workspace:**  
   You will be given the option to import your existing VS Code or Cursor settings (since Antigravity's IDE is built on a VS Code foundation). Select your preferred theme, optionally choose any "Build with Google" plugins (like Firebase or Android integrations), and click **Finish**.  
   

   ### **macOS**

   **1.Download the App:**  
   Go to [antigravity.google](https://antigravity.google) and download the macOS version.  
   **2.Install to Applications:**  
   Open your Downloads folder and run the installer. Make sure the application is moved to your **Applications** folder so it can run properly and update smoothly.  
   **3.Launch and Authenticate:**  
   Open the app from your Applications folder. You will be prompted to sign in with your Google account to activate the IDE.  
   **4.Initial Setup:**  
   Accept the Security policy, pick your theme, and import any existing editor settings you want to carry over to get started.  
   

   ### **Linux (Ubuntu)**

   **1.Download the Linux Bundle:**  
   Visit [antigravity.google](https://antigravity.google) and download the Linux .tar.gz binary bundle.  
   **2.Extract the Archive:**  
   Open your terminal (Ctrl \+ Alt \+ T), navigate to the folder where you downloaded the file, and extract the bundle using the following command (replace with your exact filename):

```shell
tar -xzf antigravity-linux-x64.tar.gz
```

   **3.Run the Setup Launcher:**Linux handles the rest automatically.

   Navigate into the extracted folder and run the setup launcher. On Linux, this launcher automatically configures the full Antigravity IDE environment natively.

   **4.Launch and Login:**

   Once installed, you will see an integrated Antigravity icon in your application menu. Launch it, authenticate with your Google account, and set up your theme.

**Resources already allocated for you**

1. 🔑 Email and Password: this  have already been shared with you on the email address you shared while registering for the event  
     
 


### Login

1. 👤 Login to GCP with your designated email to gcp  
2. 🐙 Login to Github using your account

### GCP setup

1. ☁️ Login to gcp using this link: [https://console.cloud.google.com](https://console.cloud.google.com), you should see a project with your name on it on successful login

   ![][image4]

*Login into GCP console*

Confirm you can access your Google Cloud project , the project will be named according to your name; i.e 

### ![][image5]

*GCP  Project*

2. Install gcloud cli to enable us to run gcloud commands from our local machine:  
   **Part 1: Installing the Google Cloud CLI**

   ### **Windows**

   **1.Download the Installer:**  
   Go to the [Google Cloud CLI documentation](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install) and download the **Google Cloud CLI installer** for Windows.  
   **2.Run the Setup:**Let it handle the dependencies.  
   Open the downloaded .exe file. Keep the default options checked (especially the one that says "Install Bundled Python" if you don't already have Python installed, as gcloud requires it).  
   **3.Finish and Launch:**  
   At the end of the setup, make sure "Run gcloud init" is checked and click Finish. This will automatically open a new terminal window to start the login process.  
   

   ### **macOS**

   **1.Download the Archive:**  
   Head to the [Google Cloud CLI install page](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install) and download the macOS archive. **Note:** Make sure you pick the right one for your chip (Apple Silicon for M1/M2/M3/M4 Macs, or x86\_64 for older Intel Macs).  
   **2.Extract the Folder:**  
   Open your Downloads folder and double-click the downloaded .tar.gz file to extract it. This will create a folder named google-cloud-sdk.  
   **3.Relocate the Folder:**Move it out of Downloads.  
   Drag the google-cloud-sdk folder into your Home folder (the one with your name and a little house icon) so it doesn't get accidentally deleted later.  
   **4.Run the Installer:**Run the provided installation script.  
   Open your Terminal (Cmd \+ Space, type Terminal). Drag the install.sh file from inside your new google-cloud-sdk folder directly into the Terminal window and press **Enter**. Press Y when it asks to update your PATH and enable command completion.  
   **5.Restart Terminal:**  
   Close your Terminal completely and open a fresh one so the changes take effect.  
   

   ### **Linux (Ubuntu)**

   ### Just like with VS Code, Ubuntu makes this incredibly easy using the Snap store.

```shell
Press Ctrl + Alt + T.
```

	**2.Install via Snap:**Using the Snap package manager.  
Type the following command and press Enter (enter your password when prompted):  
	

```shell
sudo snap install google-cloud-cli --classic
```

**Part 2: Logging In and Running Commands**  
Once the CLI is installed on their machine, they need to connect it to their Google Cloud account.

### **Step 1: Initialize and Login**

### Have your beginners open their terminal (or Command Prompt on Windows) and run this single command:

```shell
gcloud init
```

	This command acts as a setup wizard:

1. It will ask if you want to log in. Type `Y` and press Enter.  
2. A web browser will automatically pop open asking them to sign in to their Google account.  
3. After clicking "Allow" in the browser, they can go back to their terminal.  
4. The terminal will list their available Google Cloud projects. They just type the number corresponding to the project they want to work on and hit Enter.

	

**Step 2: Essential Beginner Commands**  
Once they are logged in, here are the core commands they will actually use on a day-to-day basis:

| Command | What it does |
| :---- | :---- |
| gcloud auth login | Forces a new browser login (useful if their session expires or they need to switch accounts). |
| gcloud projects list | Shows a list of all cloud projects attached to their account. |
| gcloud config set project \[PROJECT\_ID\] | Switches their active terminal session to a different project. |
| gcloud info | Displays their current environment details, active account, and current project. |

If they ever get stuck, they can add \--help to the end of any command (like gcloud projects \--help) to get a built-in manual explaining

how to use it.

### Github setup

1. Navigate to GitHub and select the Sign in with Google option if your account is linked to your Google account   
2. Select your work or personal email (e.g., eddie@training.cloudpartner.africa).  
3. Click Continue to authorize GitHub to access your basic profile information.  
   ![][image6]

*Sign in to Github*

4. Navigate to the Source Repository for the Cloud Mastery 2026 and proceed to 🍴 fork the repository.Forking creates a personal copy of the project under your own GitHub account. This allows you to make changes without affecting the original codebase.The repo contains our backend and frontend that we'll be deploying:  [https://github.com/Pawa-IT-Solutions/cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026](https://github.com/Pawa-IT-Solutions/cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026)   
* Locate the Fork button at the top right of the page (next to the Star button).

![][image7]  
 *Access the cloud mastery-2026 repository and initiate a fork to your github account*

* Click the dropdown and select Create a new fork.  
* On the "**Create a new fork"** page:  
* Owner: Ensure your personal username (*e.g., eddie582*) is selected.  
* Repository Name: Keep this as cloud-mastery-2026  
* Keep the **Copy the main branch only** checked  
* Click the green **Create fork** button.

![][image8]  
*Create new fork for the cloud Master 2026 repository*

5. Now that you have a copy of the repository under your account (it should now say your-username/cloud-mastery-2026 at the top left), you need to download it to your local machine.  
   * Click the green **\<\> Code** button on your forked page.  
   * Copy the HTTPS or SSH URL provided.

![][image9]  
*Clone the codebase*

* Open your terminal or command prompt within your computer and run the following command: **Note** replace the url with the one you copied from your  github repository.


```shell
git clone https://github.com/ [YOUR USERNAME] /cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026
```

6. 📂 On your IDE open the project to view the code. You can use your preferred IDE  such as VS Code or You can use Google Antigravity.

![][image10]  
 *Opening the project folder from your IDE* 

Next we’ll be setting up the secrets we’ll be using in our project:

1. 🤝 Setup workload identity federation between your Google Cloud Project and Github  
2. 🔐 Setup Github actions secrets within your Github project.

To Setup the Secrets to use within your Github project from gcp you will need to get the following 

1. 🆔 PROJECT\_ID: this is found on the GCP Welcome page : [https://console.cloud.google.com/welcome](https://console.cloud.google.com/welcome)  
2. 🔢 PROJECT\_NUMBER: You can find this also on the GCP welcome page.

   ![][image11]

*Getting the GCP project number and project ID under the GCP Welcome page*

3. 🐙 GITHUB\_REPO: Your forked repository : [https://github.com/ \[YOUR USERNAME\] /cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026](https://github.com/Pawa-IT-Solutions/cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026)  
   

![][image12]  
*Sample Github repository*

Required environment parameters:

```shell
GITHUB_REPO = [USER_NAME]/cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026
```

### Database Connection

Within your Google CLoud Project you will be creating our database instance url: [https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances](https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances)

1. Enable Cloud sql apis

   ![][image13]

*Enable cloudsql api*

2. Select Create instance  
   ![][image14]

*Create cloudsql instance*

3. Choose Mysql database

![][image15]  
*Select Database engine*

4. Enable the required apis

![][image16]  
*Enable compute engine api*

5. Create database instance  
- Choose enterprise version  
- In the select ‘Edition preset’ choose sandbox  
- For the instance info use:  
  - MySQL 8.4  
  - Name  it ‘cloud-mastery-db-v8’  
  - Set a password for the \`root\` user

![][image17]  
*Configure Database instance*

- In the \`Choose region and zonal availability\`

        \-    Choose \`us-central1\` for region

        \-    In select \`single zone\` in zonal availability

![][image18]  
*Set database region and availability*

- Click \`Create instance\` button to begin the setup

6. After successful deployment we should have a running instance

![][image19]  
*Created database instance*

7. Next we’ll setup our user and password  
- Access the users section for the Cloud SQL instance   
- Click the **Add user account button**

![][image20]  
*Add User account for the Cloud SQL instance*

* With the resulting Side Panel, select **Built-in Authentication**  
  * Create a Username and Save the username: use **Cloud\_Mastery1**  
  * For the Password use : **Cloud\_Mastery2**  
  * Once done click the **Add** button to save this credentials for the database 

![][image21]  
*Create database username and password*

8. We’ll be seeding our database with some data.  
     
- Next we select our instance so that we can import the database  
- Click on overview, then import data, and proceed to the next step  
  ![][image22]

*Database instance overview*

- Set source as \`SQL\`  
- Select source as \`Cloud Storage\`, click browse, click create bucket icon on the top right to create the bucket 


| ![][image23] | ![][image24] |
| :---- | :---- |


  *Create cloud storage bucket*

- We’ll be creating a bucket to store our sql dump file for seeding the database  
- Give the bucket a unique name eg ‘cloud-mastery-pawait’  
- Select a region where our bucket will reside. Eg use us-central-1  
- The select create button at the bottom  
  ![][image25]

*Select bucket region*

3

9. Ensure the bucket has public access by unselecting \`**Enforce public access prevention on this bucket**\`  
     
   ![][image26]

*Enable cloud storage bucket public access*

10. Navigate and select cloud storage \> bucket from the navigation menu to see our created bucket  
    ![][image27]

 *cloud storage*  
![][image28]  
*Created cloud storage bucket*

11. Next we’ll be uploading the sql dump file from the repository we cloned earlier: Click on upload \> Choose files  
    **Path:** /cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026/backend/prisma/sql-dump.sql  
    ![][image29]

*Upload the sql seed-dump to cloud storage*

12. Import the sql dump file to be used for populating the database

![][image30]

- Navigate back to cloudsql and select our instance  
- Click import data and select the file in the bucket we just created  
  ![][image31]


*Final import form*

13. After upload is successful we should be able to login into our database instance to see the tables that have been created.

| ![][image32] | ![][image33] |
| :---- | :---- |

*Select Cloud Sql*  
![][image34]

*Login to the database*

![][image35]  
*Final deployed cloudsql database*

14. Double Click on the Cloud SQL instance ID  
    \- Under the overview page scroll down and under the Connect to this instance section:  
    \- Copy the connection name: i.e *eddie-ngugi-3a56:us-central1:cloud-mastery-db-v8*

![][image36]  
*Database connection name*

15. Under the  Database section 📂 Copy the database name i.e *cloud\_mastery\_sample*

![][image37]  
*Sample database in our instance*

Required environment parameters

```shell
DB_NAME = [Database name]
DB_PASS = [Created password]
DB_USER = [Created user]
CLOUDSQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION_NAME = [Connection name]
```

### Workload Identity Federation in GCP

#### What is workload identity federation?

Workload Identity Federation is a secure method for granting external identities (like GitHub Actions) temporary access to Google Cloud resources without using long-lived service account keys. It works by having Google Cloud trust an external identity provider (GitHub) and then mapping the external identity's token to a specific GCP Service Account. This process eliminates the security risks associated with storing static keys, ensuring a secure and keyless CI/CD pipeline for deploying applications to Cloud Run.

Steps:

1. 🐚 On your GCP console click on cloud shell to activate it. This is where we’ll be running our commands.  
   

![][image38]  
*Activate cloudShell to run commands on GCP*

1.1 Click Authorize on the pop screen

On gcloud console to setup:  
![][image39]  
*Sample GCP Cloud Shell*

1. ⚙️ Set your variables  
   First, define these in your terminal so the commands work correctly:

1.1 On Cloud Shell, Linux or Mac OS

```shell
export PROJECT_ID="" 
export GITHUB_REPO="" 
export PROJECT_NUMBER=""
```

1.2 On Windows 

```shell
set PROJECT_ID="" 
set GITHUB_REPO="" 
set PROJECT_NUMBER=""
```

2. Create the Workload Identity Pool  
   2.1 On Cloud Shell, Linux or Mac OS  
   You must have a "Pool" container before you can add GitHub as a provider.

```shell
gcloud iam workload-identity-pools create "github-pool-v1" \
    --project="$PROJECT_ID" \
    --location="global" \
    --display-name="GitHub Pool V1"
```

   2.2 On Windows 

```shell
gcloud iam workload-identity-pools create "github-pool-v1" --project="%PROJECT_ID%" --location="global" --display-name="GitHub Pool V1"
```

3. 🆔 Create the OIDC Provider  
   This command tells Google Cloud to trust GitHub's identity tokens.  
- Change assertion.repository\_owner \== ‘\[your github username\]’

3.1  On Cloud Shell, Linux or Mac OS

```shell
gcloud iam workload-identity-pools providers create-oidc "github-provider-v1" \
    --project="$PROJECT_ID" \
    --location="global" \
    --workload-identity-pool="github-pool-v1" \
    --display-name="GitHub Provider V1" \
    --issuer-uri="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \
--attribute-mapping="google.subject=assertion.sub,attribute.repository=assertion.repository,attribute.actor=assertion.actor" \
    --attribute-condition="assertion.repository_owner == 'Pawa-IT-Solutions'"
```

3.2 On Windows 

```shell
gcloud iam workload-identity-pools providers create-oidc "github-provider-v1" --project="%PROJECT_ID%" --location="global" --workload-identity-pool="github-pool-v1" --display-name="GitHub Provider V1" --issuer-uri="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" --attribute-mapping="google.subject=assertion.sub,attribute.repository=assertion.repository,attribute.actor=assertion.actor" --attribute-condition="assertion.repository_owner == 'Pawa-IT-Solutions'"
```

4. Next we need to bind github with the workload identity pool

	This allows github to establish a connection with our GCP project

4.1 On Cloud Shell, Linux or Mac OS

```shell
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding "github-deploy-sa@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
--project="YOUR_PROJECT_ID" \
--role="roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" \
--member="principalSet://iam.googleapis.com/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-pool-v1/attribute.repository/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026"
```

4.2  On Windows 

```shell
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding "github-deploy-sa@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com" ^
--project="YOUR_PROJECT_ID" ^
--role="roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" ^
--member="principalSet://iam.googleapis.com/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-pool-v1/attribute.repository/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026"

```

5. Open the shell script on the root of our cloned repo \`[setup-github-wif.sh](http://setup-github-wif.sh)\`. We’ll be editing the variable \`GITHUB\_OWNER\` to match your repository using our IDE.  
- On linux and mac OS use  
  **Path: /**cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026/s[etup-github-wif.sh](http://setup-github-wif.sh)  
- On Windows use

  **Path:  /**cloud-mastery-ecommerce-2026/s[etup-github-wif.](http://setup-github-wif.sh)bat


![][image40]\\  
*Sample GCP Cloud Shell*

- Edit \`GITHUB\_OWNER\` to match your repository (Linux and MacOS)  
  ![][image41]

*Change ‘GITHUB\_OWNER’ value*

- On windows machines 

![][image42]  
*Change ‘GITHUB\_OWNER’ value*

6. On our terminal we’ll login to google cloud to run commands for the initial setup of our project using the script above  
   1\.  Login with the email you were given  earlier on linux/macos **terminal.**   
        On Windows use **command prompt**

```shell
gcloud auth application-default login
```

2\.  On linux modify the script permissions and run it using the commands below

```shell
chmod +x setup-github-wif.sh
./setup-github-wif.sh
```

3\. On Windows run the following command

```shell
setup-github-wif.bat
```

4\. Wait for the script to finalize. You should see output similar to the screenshot below:


```
GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER: projects/[PROJECT_NUMBER]/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-pool-v1/providers/github-provider-v1
GCP_DEPLOYER_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL: github-deploy-sa@pawait-data-hub.iam.gserviceaccount.com
```

7. If you are using google cloud console we’ll be uploading the shell script to enable GCP apis and modify role permissions for our service account.

![][image43]  
*Upload file on cloud shell*

- After successfully uploading the file we’ll need to modify it’s permissions and run it on the cloud shell

```shell
chmod +x setup-github-wif.sh
./setup-github-wif.sh
```


  


  

8. Copy \`github-deploy\` service account

   ![][image44]

*Github service account*

9. After the script runs successfully copy these 2 values:

```shell
GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER: projects/[PROJECT_NUMBER]/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-pool-v1/providers/github-provider-v1
GCP_DEPLOYER_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL: github-deploy-sa@pawait-data-hub.iam.gserviceaccount.com
```

### 

### Automated Backend & Frontend Deployment

#### What are github actions and secrets?

GitHub Actions Secrets are critical for secure Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD). They allow the pipeline to access sensitive information; like your Google Cloud Project IDs, Service Account Emails, Workload Identity Provider details, and database credentials without exposing these values directly in the workflow files or source code. This adherence to secret management best practices ensures that all sensitive tokens are encrypted within GitHub and used only when necessary, preventing unauthorized access and maintaining security.

#### Steps: 

1. 🔐 Setup github secrets that will be used to run our CI/CD

	2.1 Click on the settings tab  
	2.2 Click on the ‘Security and quality’ in the navigation menu  
	2.3 Click ‘Actions’ in the Secrets and variables drop down menu  
![][image45]  
*Github Actions Secrets*

- Variables we’ll be saving to run our project


```shell
   GCP_PROJECT_ID = ""
GCP_REGION= us-central1
GCP_WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_PROVIDER= projects/[PROJECT_NUMBER]/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-pool-v1/providers/github-provider-v1
GCP_DEPLOYER_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL= github-deploy-sa@[PROJECT_ID].iam.gserviceaccount.com
CLOUD_RUN_BACKEND_SERVICE_NAME= soko-backend
CLOUD_RUN_FRONTEND_SERVICE_NAME= soko-frontend
CLOUDSQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION_NAME= ""
DB_NAME= ""
DB_USER= ""
DB_PASSWORD= ""
MYSQL_PRISMA_URL= mysql://Cloud_Mastery1:Cloud_Mastery2@localhost:3306/cloud_mastery_sample?socket=/cloudsql/CLOUDSQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION_NAME
FRONTEND_NEXT_PUBLIC_CART_API_URL= https://checkout-service-[PROJECT_NUMBER].us-central1.run.app
FRONTEND_NEXT_PUBLIC_CHAT_DEPLOYMENT= projects/659303448426/locations/us/apps/95764a9d-bd50-46a3-a79d-f5496d6b3296/deployments/9dbb73bd-cdf6-473c-889a-22f2dc5077e8

FRONTEND_NEXT_PUBLIC_CHAT_TITLE= SokoAI Agent
PESAPAL_CONSUMER_KEY= qkio1BGGYAXTu2JOfm7XSXNruoZsrqEW    
PESAPAL_CONSUMER_SECRET= osGQ364R49cXKeOYSpaOnT++rHs=    
PESAPAL_NOTIFICATION_ID= 59334f6f-a2ca-442f-91d2-da2b58d3e152
```

- Expected Result:

![][image46]  
*Github Actions Prefilled Secrets*

2. On Google Console search for ‘Secret manager’ and confirm a  secret was created  in secret manager to store our Database URL to be used by cloud run

```shell
MYSQL_PRISMA_URL = mysql://Cloud_Mastery1:Cloud_Mastery2@localhost:3306/cloud_mastery_sample?socket=/cloudsql/[CLOUDSQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION_NAME]
```

![][image47]  
*GCP Secrets manager*

3. ⚙️ Checkout the deployed Github workflow that we’ll be using to deploy our backend and frontend applications

![][image48]  
*Github CI/DC workflow*  
3.1 Our pipeline is split in 2 stages: to deploy our application and the three cloud functions  
![][image49]

4. 🚀 Make a change on the [README.md](http://README.md) file and push change to the repository   
   1. ⚡ This will trigger the pipeline to deploy the application in our GCP project  
5. Interact with the deployed application on the link on cloud run.

### Expected Results

1. ☁️ 6 running cloud run services  
   1. 🌐 2 services for the frontend and backend  
   2. 🌐 4 pre-deployed services  
2. 💾 Deployed and seeded Cloud sql instance  
3. On Google Cloud Console search for **‘Cloud Run’**  
   **![][image50]**

*Search and select Cloud run*

- Click on ‘Services’ to see the deployed application and cloud functions.

![][image51]  
*Deployed Cloud run services*

4. As a final step we need to manually run the function **\`sync-mysql-to-bq\`** which populates our big query dataset from our cloudsql instance.  
     
   ![][image52]

     
   4.1 Wait for the process to complete upon which you’ll see this success message.  
     
   ![][image53] 

*Sync cloudsql with Big query*

5. Below is the front end application fully deployed and running. You can access your deployed application using soko-frontend url service.

*![][image54]*
